summary: I was born in Guinea on May 7, 1995, specifically in the capital city. Growing up in Guinea shaped much of my early life and culture, especially being part of the Sosso ethnicity. My experiences there, including family and community ties, remained close to my heart even after I moved abroad. Leaving Guinea was difficult, but it was always my dream to come to the United States.

Conakry is the capital city of Guinea where I was born and raised. I spent my childhood there, surrounded by family and friends, and it was the place where my dreams of coming to the U.S. began. Even after moving away, I often thought about Conakry and the life I had there. It was home to me until I left to pursue my studies and new opportunities.

The United States has always been my dream destination since I was young. My mom and I used to visit here for vacations, and I loved the fun times we had. After my mom passed away in 2012, I worked with my dad to make my dream of moving to the U.S. a reality. I finally arrived about a year and a half ago, living with my sisters and adjusting to a new life focused on studying and building a future.
cues: arrived one and a half years ago

summary: I visited my sister in Albany, Georgia for four months after moving to the U.S. It was part of my early experience adjusting to life here. Albany was a place where I could connect with family and feel supported while I was still getting used to everything. Those months helped me gain strength and confidence to continue pursuing my dreams in the U.S.

I belong to the Sosso people, which is my ethnic group in Guinea. Being Sosso is an important part of my identity and heritage. Growing up in Conakry, my culture and traditions from the Sosso community influenced my upbringing and values. Even after moving to the U.S., I carry my Sosso roots with me, especially through my traditional African clothes that I brought along.
